coredata.dc:title: High efficiency spin-valve and spin-filter in a doped rhombic graphene quantum dot device
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
coredata.dc:creator: Silva, P.V.
Saraiva-Souza, A.
Maia, D.W.
Souza, F.M.
Filho, A.G. Souza
Meunier, V.
Girão, E.C.
coredata.dc:description:
Spin-polarized transport through a rhombic graphene quantum dot (rGQD) attached to armchair graphene nanoribbon (AGNR) electrodes is investigated by means of the Green’s function technique combined with single-band tight-binding (TB) approach including a Hubbard-like term. The Hubbard repulsion was included within the mean-field approximation. Compared to anti-ferromagnetic (AFM), we show that the ferromagnetic (FM) ordering of the rGQD corresponds to a smaller bandgap, thus resulting in an efficient spin injector. As a consequence, the electron transport spectrum reveals a spin valve effect, which is controlled by doping with B/N atoms creating a p-n-type junction. The calculations point out that such systems can be used as spin-filter devices with efficiency close to a 100
%
.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rhombic-GQD
11-AGNR
Molecular electronics
NEGF-DFT
Electron transport

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Schematic representation of AGNR/rGNF/AGNR two-probe system. Dopant positions
d
1
and
d
2
are distinguished by marked atoms in the left (
z
- ∞
) and right (
z
+ ∞
) region at different sublattices. The configuration I corresponds to
d
1
=
d
2
= C and II to
d
1
= B and
d
2
= N, representing the undoped and doped systems, respectively. The C atoms are colored in gray and the dopant in white.

(a), (b) and (c) The electronic band structures of undoped, B-doped, and N-doped 11-AGNR electrodes, respectively. (d), (e) and (f) Energy level diagram for spin-up (full black lines) and -down (dashed red lines) states and frontier molecular orbitals (HOMO-LUMO) for the rGQD in the NM, AFM and FM magnetic configurations, respectively. The atoms are colored in gray for C, yellow for B and blue for N; orbitals are plotted with a maximum contour value of 0.03 au.

Spin-polarized transmission spectra of 11-AGNR/rGQD/11-AGNR. (a), (b) and (c) the undoped device (
d
1
=
d
2
= C) for NM, AFM and FM configuration, respectively. (d) the doped device with
d
1
= B and
d
2
= N. The black dashed line for spin-up and red solid line for spin-down.

(a) Schematic two-probe system of B-11AGNR/
n · d
rGQD/N-11AGNR where the rhombic GQD is increasingly co-doped and doped by (B,N), where n and d stands for the number and type of dopant atom(s). (b) and (c) Transmission spectra for the spin-up and spin-down component of B-11AGNR/
n ·
(B,N)-rGQD/N-11AGNR, respectively. The atoms are colored in C (dark gray), B (pink) and N (blue).

(a), (b) Schematic two-probe system of B-11AGNR/
n · d
rGQD/N-11AGNR where the rhombic GQD is increasingly co-doped and doped by (B,N), where n and d stands for the number and type of dopant atom(s). (c) and (d) Transmission spectra for the spin-up component of B-11AGNR/
n ·
B-rGQD/N-11AGNR and B-AGNR/
n ·
N-rGQD/N-AGNR, respectively. In the same order, the transmission spectra for spin-down component are in (e) and (f). The atoms are colored in C (dark gray), B (pink) and N (blue).

Isosurface plots of local density of states (LDOS) plotted for particular energies from the transmission spectrum of
n ·
B-rGQD (n = 1,3 and 5). For spin-up (panel left), values below and at
E
F
are considered, while the for spin-down (panel right), values above and at
E
F
are plotted. The isovalue for the surface is 0.15 a.u.
Spin-filtering efficiency (SFE) for electrons as a function of the numbers of B and/or N dopants at the rGQD.

head.ce:title: High efficiency spin-valve and spin-filter in a doped rhombic graphene quantum dot device

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Física, Universidade Federal do Ceará, CEP 60455-900, P.O. Box 6030, Fortaleza, Ceará, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Física, Universidade Federal do Piauí, CEP 64049-550, Teresina, Piauí, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instituto de Física, Universidade Federal de Uberlândia, CEP 38400-902, Uberlândia, Minas Gerais, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Applied Physics, and Astronomy, Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, Troy, NY, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Spin-polarized transport through a rhombic graphene quantum dot (rGQD) attached to armchair graphene nanoribbon (AGNR) electrodes is investigated by means of the Green’s function technique combined with single-band tight-binding (TB) approach including a Hubbard-like term. The Hubbard repulsion was included within the mean-field approximation. Compared to anti-ferromagnetic (AFM), we show that the ferromagnetic (FM) ordering of the rGQD corresponds to a smaller bandgap, thus resulting in an efficient spin injector. As a consequence, the electron transport spectrum reveals a spin valve effect, which is controlled by doping with B/N atoms creating a p-n-type junction. The calculations point out that such systems can be used as spin-filter devices with efficiency close to a 100
%
.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based molecular devices have attracted increasing attention due to their unique electronic and magnetic properties[1–3]. Compared to metal electrode systems, carbon devices exhibit large electromechanical stability and mechanical flexibility, ensuring greater control over the interface between molecule and electrode[4–7]. Moreover, their long spin-coherence time is desirable for quantum information processing implementations [8,9]. Theoretical[10–12] studies and experimental[13–16] reports have shown that the magnetic properties of a number of graphene-based materials are highly sensitive to their edge geometries. Notably, carbon-based materials with zigzag edges possess magnetic ordering[17,18], while systems with armchair edges do not show any magnetization [19].

Another class of graphene based structures which also show relevant magnetic configurations in their ground state are graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are nanometer-sized planar fragments of graphene. Especially, GQDs present the photo-induced electron transfer property, which might be used to increase efficiency of solar cells and optoelectronics components [38–41]. Several studies have been carried out to investigate the electronic and magnetic properties for different shaped GQDs [42–45]. The magnetization of GQDs, present in the case of an unequal number of interpenetrating A and B sublattices (Lieb’s theorem [27]), is exploited in many devices with intriguing physical properties, such as long-range electron tunneling [46,47], current switching [48,49], field-effect characteristics [50,51], current rectification [52–54], and spin filter effect [55,56].

In this paper, we report a theoretical study of the electronic structure and spin-polarized transport properties in a two-probe system consisting of a rhombic GQD (rGQD) sandwiched between nonmagnetic armchair graphene nanoribbons (AGNRs) electrodes. In order to explore the magnetic properties of the isolated rGQD, we examine this structure with different magnetic configurations. Despite their different electronic bandgaps, the frontier orbital states possess distinct characteristics. In particular, the ferromagnetic ordering has a smaller bandgap and it is more suitable to be used as an efficient spin injector. Based on this configuration, spin-dependent transport is observed. More importantly, a nearly perfect spin filter effect is found considering different substitutional dopants (B and N atoms). Doping gives rise to a semiconductor-to-metal transition accompanied by substantial changes of the spin-dependent transmission spectra, thus suggesting the possibility of their use as spin-filter devices with an efficiency close to 100
%
.

We investigate the spin-polarized transport in a two-probe system as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The central region includes a rGQD which is coupled to two semi-infinite 11-AGNR electrodes which extend to the left and right directions of the scattering region. In order to keep smooth electronic charge between the electrodes and the central region, seven buffer layers are included in the central region. We investigate two configurations which differ from each other by the type of doping
d
1
and
d
2
on the left and right leads, respectively (see Fig. 1). As the graphene honeycomb lattice is composed by two interpenetrating triangular sub-lattices A and B, the doping atoms are located in the same C–C dimer line from the AGNRs, but on different sublattices (A =
d
1
and B =
d
2
). The configurations are identified as I (
d
1
=
d
2
= C, undoped) and II (
d
1
= B and
d
2
= N, doped) systems.

The Hamiltonian describing the system sketched in Fig. 1 is written as
H =
H
0
+
H
′
, where
H
0
is the usual tight-binding Hamiltonian given by
(1)
H
0
=
∑
j , σ
∊
j
| j , σ 〉 〈 j , σ | +
∑
i , j , σ
γ
ji
| j , σ 〉 〈 i , σ | ,
where
| j , σ 〉
stands for the atomic orbital with spin
σ
centered on atom j. Here the Hamiltonian is restricted to the
π
-orbitals from the C, B, and N atoms.
∊
j
and
γ
ji
are the on-site energy and hopping integrals, respectively. Those parameters adopt an appropriate parametrization which includes carbon–carbon interactions up to third-nearest-neighbors [57]. For simplicity, we set carbon-boron and carbon–nitrogen hopping integrals as being the same as those for carbon–carbon interactions, while on-site energies are set either above (B-doping case) or below (N-doping case) carbon’s site energy. These
∊
B
and
∊
N
values relative to
∊
C
= 0
are chosen to be similar to renormalized on-site energies proposed for the study of N and B doping on carbon nanotubes [58]. Such choice results in bandstructures for B and N doped AGNRs in good agreement with DFT results [30].

The Coulomb interaction written in the atomic orbital basis can be cast in the Hubbard term in a mean-field fashion as
(2)
H ′ = U
∑
j
〈
n
̂
j
↑
〉
n
̂
j
↓
+ 〈
n
̂
j
↓
〉
n
̂
j
↑
,
where
〈
n
̂
j
σ
〉
is the average occupation for site
j ,
n
̂
j
σ
is the number operator, and
σ = ↑ , ↓
is the spin component. The parameter U is the on-site Coulomb repulsion strength for which we used
U = 2.944
eV as proposed earlier [59]. From the theoretical point of view, the value of U cannot be directly compared to different simulation methods. However, it is possible to compare the dimensionless parameter
U / γ
, where
γ
is the first nearest neighbor interaction term [60]. Results from DFT calculations performed using local-spin-density approximation are best reproduced when
U / γ = 0.9
[17,18]. Therefore, we choose an optimal value of U that corresponds to
U / γ = 0.92
. Moreover, quantum Monte Carlo simulations correctly described the honeycomb lattice of graphene with
U / γ
ranging from 0.9 to 1.3 [61]. On the other hand, magnetic resonance studies of neutral soliton states in trans-polyacetylene gives U values between
≈
3.0 and 3.5 eV [62,63].

For the electronic transport calculations, we used a combination of the Landauer formalism [64] and the Green’s function technique [65]. Landauer’s formula relates quantum conductance
G
with transmission spectrum T through
(3)
G =
e
2
h
T .

Transmission is computed through the Green’s function approach. The retarded/advanced Green’s function is given by
(4)
G
r , a
=
[ E I ± i η - H -
Σ
L
( E ) -
Σ
R
( E ) ]
- 1
,
where
Σ
L , R
are self-energy matrices which are obtained through an iterative procedure [66,67] and the computation of
G
r , a
is made by means of a set of iterative algorithms [68,69]. The
η
quantity is a small energy term (
=
10
- 6
eV) used to ensure causality in the calculation of
Σ
L , R
. The transmission coefficient is obtained from the standard result [65]
(5)
T
σ
( E ) = Tr [
Γ
L
G
r
Γ
R
G
a
] ,
where the coupling matrix
Γ
( L / R )
is found via the self-energy according to
(6)
Γ
( L / R )
= i [
Σ
( L / R )
( E ) -
Σ
( L / R )
†
( E ) ] .

AGNRs are known to display a semiconductor behavior [12,14] while isolated rGQD possess magnetic states [70]. The relevant point remains to know how the transport properties are affected by the coupling between rGQD and 11-AGNRs electrode with undoped and BN doped GQDs. We first consider the electronic structure of the electrodes and central region separately (see Fig. 2 ). In Figs. 2(a)-(c) we show the band structure for a periodic 11-AGNR in the horizontal z-direction. For a undoped 11-AGNR, a narrow bandgap of 0.11 eV is found. This bandgap might be tuned by using B(N) atoms, thus yielding a similar behavior of p-(n-) type semiconductors [30]. As a consequence, the energy levels shift up (down) to valence (conduction) bands since holes (electrons) are introduced by acceptor (donor) B (N) doping. Both doped systems present bands that clearly cross the Fermi energy close to the
Γ
point, which might allow a high rectification in electronic devices based on AGNR doped electrodes.

In Fig. 2(d)–(f), we show the energy level diagram, the highest occupied molecular orbital-lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (HOMO-LUMO) for the three magnetic states of the rGQD. The black dashed (red) line denotes the spin-up (spin-dw) contribution. The non-magnetic (NM) configuration presents a small HOMO-LUMO energy gap of
≈
0.29 eV, while the AFM configuration have highest energy gap of
≈
0.41 eV. Analyzing the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) sheds light on the origin of these energy gaps. For NM states the HOMO-LUMO density is distributed over the entire structure of the rGQD and both spin components show similar distribution. In the AFM configuration the LDOS corresponding to the HOMO accumulates on the left side (Fig. 2(e)-bottom), while the LUMO has its LDOS mainly distributed at the opposite side (Fig. 2(e)-top). Additionally, spin-up states are mainly located at the left side of the rGQD, while the opposite spin-down remains at the right side (not shown). Thus, the AFM configuration presents spin polarization along the edge, with a zero net spin magnetization. Moreover, the AFM alignment of the edge spin states emerges as the ground state of the system. The FM configuration shows two important features: I) the molecular orbital distribution is similar to NM configuration, and II) the band diagram shows an
≈
0.20 eV energy gap. However, in contrast with the NM case, the HOMO energy level is composed exclusively by spin-down states, while the opposite spin-down component is suppressed. This gives rise to a spin-splitting of the energy levels, with the HOMO(LUMO) being polarized with spin-down(-up). This result agrees quite well with previous work [71]. In practice, the high energy gap might represent an “off” state while the low energy gap represents an “on” state [25]. Coupling the rGQD to the 11-AGNR can lead in a high performance device. We investigate this effect in Figs. 3 (a)-(d), where we show the spin-resolved transmission coefficients
T
σ
( E )
for NM, AFM and FM configurations, respectively. In the NM and AFM configurations, we find a semiconductor system with two peaks around the Fermi energy with maximal transmission per spin given by
T
up
( E ) =
T
dw
( E ) ≈ 0.2
, meaning that no spin polarized transport arises into the system. In the FM alignment, however, the transmission spectra near the
E
F
is quite different. The transmission around [
± 0.1 ; ± 0.5
] eV corresponds to the two broad peaks with one of having a value close to
T
σ
( E ) ≈ 1
, with opposite spin when moving from occupied to unoccupied states. The negative energy shows only spin-up contributions for the transmission, while the positive energy is dominated by spin-down. The spin-splitting brings an interesting spin-valve transport behavior.

In Fig. 3(d) we plot the transmission spectra for the II case (doped leads) as we show in Fig. 1 (
d
1
= B and
d
2
= N) for the FM configuration. The
T
σ
( E )
is modified with respect to the undoped system (Fig. 3(c)). Here the transmission exhibits three peaks, with a sharp peak near
E
F
, so that the gap is slightly reduced compared to the undoped case. Yet,
E
F
lies inside the band gap, thus resulting in a semiconductor device. The transmission shows significant amplitudes in the [
± 0.08 ; ± 0.5
] eV range, preserving the spin valve characteristic from the undoped case, but with transmission having slightly different values when compared to unoccupied states.

It would be highly desirable to close the gap for a single spin component, keeping the opposite spin blocked, thereby creating a semi-metal. This condition can result in a metallic system for a single spin component, so that the system could in principle operate as a highly efficient spin filtering diode, thus allowing a fully spin-polarized current for positive bias voltage and zero current for reverse bias. For this reason, electron transport calculations were also carried out on a two-probe model similar to the Fig. 1, with
d
1
= B and
d
2
= N, but now with dopant atoms placed in the central scattering region. Three systems were considered:
n ·
(B,N)-rGQD,
n ·
(B)-rGQD and
n ·
(N)-rGQD, where n is the number of substitutional dopants as shown in Fig. 4 a) and Fig. 5 (a) and (b), respectively. In all cases, doping is considered with C atoms substituted in the same sublattice of
d
1
= B and
d
2
= N, respectively, for boron and nitrogen dopants.

Fig. 4(b) shows the spin-up transmission spectra when the rhombic GQD is doped with both B and N atoms in the FM configuration (Fig. 4(a)). The transmission peaks appear in the same [
± 0.1 ; ± 0.5
] eV energy interval for
1 ·
(B,N)-rGQD and
2 ·
(B,N)-rGQD with a little shift to negative energies for
3 ·
(B,N)-rGQD. Meanwhile, the transmission spectrum for spin-down component (Fig. 4(c)) remains in the opposite energy interval. By comparing this transmission with that of the undoped system (Fig. 3(d)) we see that the transmission does not significantly change near
E
F
for both spin components, as might be expected. Surprisingly however, when the rGQD is doped only with B on N atoms, as we show in Fig. 5(a) and (b), the transmission coefficient can become highly polarized around
E
F
depending on the dopant. In Fig. 5 (b) and (e) for
1 ·
B-rGQD both
T
up
( E )
and
T
dw
( E )
remain close to zero around
E
F
. The spin-up transmission is characterized by a shallow (sharp) peak on the left (right) of
E
F
. In the spin-down case, we note one peak with high transmission coefficient
T
dw
( E ) ≈ 1
for
E < 0
, while for positive energies a single transmission peak is found with
T
dw
( E ) ≈ 0.5
. As we increase B atom concentration on the rGQD, we note a shift on the transmission spectrum for both spin components toward positive energies (as the B atoms introduce electron holes).

For
3 ·
B-rGQD atoms the
T
dw
( E )
is already larger than
T
up
( E )
as the tail of a spin-down transmission peak reaches the Fermi level. Finally, for
5 ·
B-rGQD atoms we find
T
dw
( E )
≫
T
up
( E )
around
E
F
. In the case of N doping on the central flake, the mechanism is opposite to the boron case (transmission spectra shifting to negative energies as N atoms introduce electrons in the system), but the resulting behavior is identical. In Fig. 5(d) and (f) we plot
T
σ
( E )
for increasing concentration of N into rGQD. When we reach
5 ·
N-rGQD atoms, transmission at the Fermi level is highly polarized,
T
dw
( E )
≪
T
up
( E )
. This result suggests the possibility to selectively choose which spin component dominates the transport by p- or n-type dopant.

In order to understand this polarization effect observed on the transmission spectrum for the systems
n ·
B-rGQD we plot the local density of states (LDOS) for three concentrations of B atoms (1B, 3B and 5B) at certain energy points from the Figs. 5 (c) and (d). The LDOS was also taken into account in the
n ·
N-rGQD which exhibits an opposite LDOS distribution compared to
n ·
B-rGQD. Fig. 6 (left panel) shows the spin-up LDOS contribution for the peaks on the negative energies close to
E
F
, namely −0.13 eV for
1 ·
B-rGQD (T(E)
≈
0.04), −0.07 eV for
3 ·
B-rGQD(T(E)
≈
0.03) and −0.04 eV for
5 ·
B-rGQD(T(E)
≈
0.02). In each energy point we observe a small concentration of states at location of the coupling region between the rGQD and the electrodes. Notice the absence of spin-up states for all B concentrations at the
E
F
.

In Fig. 6 (right panel) it is shown the LDOS contribution for the spin-down component corresponding to
E
F
and the energy points above
E
F
: 0.12 eV for
1 ·
B-rGQD (T(E)
≈
0.50), 0.19 eV for
3 ·
B-rGQD (T(E)
≈
0.63) and 0.15 eV for
5 ·
B-rGQD (T(E)
≈
0.47). The energy states above
E
F
exhibit a large LDOS concentration at the coupling region between rGQD and the electrodes, corresponding to the narrow high transmission peak in Fig. 5(e). At the Fermi level we find that the LDOS shows high delocalization as the number of B dopant increases. In particular, for
5 ·
B-rGQD the LDOS for spin-down contribution is significantly enhanced at the interface between the rGQD and the electrodes. However, the electronic states are asymmetrically distributed around the coupling regions of the system, with a high concentration of states at the n-type junction. This result might indicate that the electrons transmit quite well from the left to the right electrode via the doped rGQD. A similar behavior of spin-polarized LDOS was found when nitrogen is used as dopant, where for
5 ·
N-rGQD we observed a large amplification of LDOS for spin-up at the interface between rGQD and leads, with a high concentration of states at the p-type junction.

Finally, we quantified the spin-filtering efficiency (SFE) at the Fermi level when the rGQD is doped with only one kind of dopant (since there are no states crossing
E
F
for the B-11AGNR/
n ·
(B,N)-rGQD/N-11AGNR junction). The SFE from the transmission spectrum can defined as
[ (
T
up
(
E
F
) -
T
dw
(
E
F
) ) / (
T
up
(
E
F
) +
T
dw
(
E
F
) ) ] × 100 %
, and Fig. 7 shows the variation of SFE with the number n of dopants.

One observes that increasing the number of dopants (either B or N) into the rGQD introduces a higher concentration of charge carriers and increases the spin-filtering efficiency. For instance, when n = 4 and n = 5 the SFE reaches values close to 100
%
thus indicating nearly perfect spin-filter effect. Moreover, each system
n ·
B-rGQD or
n ·
N-rGQD controls a different spin component (majority or minority) of the electronic flowing trough the device.

In summary, we studied the spin-dependent transport in a two-probe system based on carbon materials (namely, a single graphene flake connected to AGNRs) as a function of doping by boron and/or nitrogen atoms. The device is made up of 11-AGNR electrodes coupled to a rGQD used as a channel. The emerging magnetic properties originate from edge states of the rGQD, rather than the electrode, as it is usually the case. We believe this feature might be useful for the development of spintronic devices. The most important results are based on the FM configuration. This undoped system presents two broad peaks with high
T
σ
( E ) ≈ 1
which have opposite spin directions, so that this system might operate as a spin-valve device. Moreover, these two broad peaks become three narrow peaks by doping the device in the left(right) electrode with B(N) atoms, creating a
p - n
semiconductor junction. In addition, doping rGQD with B or N atoms can control these three narrow peaks relative to the Fermi energy. In that case, depending on the doping concentration, the transmission for one spin component is allowed while it is suppressed for the other spin component, thus suggesting the possibility of developing spin-filter devices with an efficiency close 100
%
. As experimental advances allow for the controlled assembly of hybrid structures attached to crystalline electrodes [72], this kind of structure possesses very interesting spin transport properties that can be exploited for the development of new molecular spintronics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: P.V.S. and D.W.M. acknowledge CAPES for scholarship support. A.S.-S. acknowledges the Brazilian agency CAPES for the post-doctoral program fellowship PNPD (Process Number 1510765). F.M.S. is grateful for the financial support from CNPq, CAPES and FAPEMIG. E.C.G. acknowledges support from CNPq (Process No. 473714/2013–2 and Process No. 306378/2014–0). E.C.G. and V.M. acknowledge support from CAPES through the Science Without Borders program (Project No. A085/2013). A.G.S.F acknowledge suport from PROCAD 2013/CAPES program and CNPQ grant. F.M.S. is grateful for the financial support from CNPq (Process No. 307464/2015-6), CAPES and FAPEMIG.
